perturbation.original_text: (e) This Agreement has been executed and delivered in Colorado Springs, Colorado and the validity and interpretation hereof shall be governed in all respects by the laws of the State of Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) This Agreement has been executed and delivered in Colorado Springs, Colorado and the validity and interpretation hereof shall be governed in all respects by the laws of another jurisdiction chosen at the CMC's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces the provision that dictates Colorado law as the governing law to say that the governing law is at the sole discretion of the CMC. This introduces legal risk because it allows the CMC to potentially select a jurisdiction that is unfavorable to the consumer and reduces predictability, potentially violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Colorado Consumer Protection Act prohibits unfair or deceptive trade practices. By unilaterally reserving the right to choose the governing law, CMC creates an imbalance of power and introduces uncertainty for the consumer, potentially disadvantaging them in legal disputes and thereby violating the spirit and possibly the letter of the CCPA.

Stat. 2024 Colorado Rev. Stat. 2023 Colorado Rev. Stat. 2022 Colorado Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Colorado Code Title 6 - Consumer and Commercial Affairs Article 1 - Colorado Consumer Protection Act Part 1 - Consumer Protection - General § 6-1-105. Unfair or Deceptive Trade Practices Universal Citation: CO Rev Stat § 6-1-105 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next A person engages in a deceptive trade practice when, in the course of the person's business, vocation, or occupation, the person: Either knowingly or recklessly passes off goods, services, or property as those of another; Either knowingly or recklessly makes a false representation as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods, services, or property; Either knowingly or recklessly makes a false representation as to affiliation, connection, or association with or certification by another; Uses deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services; Either knowingly or recklessly makes a false representation as to the characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, alterations, or quantities of goods, food, services, or property or a false representation as to the sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection of a person therewith; Represents that goods are original or new if he knows or should know that they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, reclaimed, used, or secondhand; Represents that goods, food, services, or property are of a particular standard, quality, or grade, or that goods are of a particular style or model, if he knows or should know that they are of another; Di
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the Colorado Consumer Protection Act (CCPA) by allowing the CMC to unilaterally choose the governing law, potentially selecting a jurisdiction unfavorable to the consumer. This creates an imbalance of power and uncertainty, disadvantaging the consumer in legal disputes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Colorado Revised Statutes Section 6-1-105 (2022), defines 'unfair or deceptive trade practices.' While the snippet doesn't explicitly mention choice of law, the unilateral power granted to CMC to choose the governing law introduces a potentially deceptive practice, giving them an unfair advantage by choosing laws favorable to themselves and harmful to the consumer. This weakens consumer protection and creates a high likelihood of non-compliance with the CCPA.

perturbation.original_text: Valco and Brubaker shall reimburse CMC for its expenses, including reasonable attorney's fees, incurred in connection with the enforcement of the provisions hereof relating to a breach of this Agreement by Valco or Brubaker.
perturbation.changed_text: Valco and Brubaker shall reimburse CMC for its expenses, including attorney's fees, incurred in connection with the enforcement of the provisions hereof relating to a breach of this Agreement by Valco or Brubaker.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'reasonable' before attorney's fees, the clause allows CMC to claim exorbitant attorney's fees, regardless of their actual necessity or market value. This violates the consumer's right to fair and reasonable costs associated with legal disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.5(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Rule of Professional Conduct 1.5(a) states that a lawyer shall not make an agreement for, charge, or collect an unreasonable fee or an unreasonable amount for expenses. Removing 'reasonable' contradicts this rule by potentially allowing for unreasonable attorney's fees to be charged to Valco and Brubaker.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: legal ethics | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you legal ethics Overview Legal ethics broadly refer to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system given the important role and influence they have in society. Because of their role and their close involvement in the administration of law, lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Most commonly, legal ethics refers to these rules of professional responsibility : the actual responsibilities lawyers must follow by law such as client confidentiality. However, legal ethics can also refer to the discussion on broader moral principles that societies place on lawyers that may not be legally required. Professional Responsibility The law governing the professional responsibility of lawyers, since they are admitted to practice by states, is largely governed by the law of each state. However, all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA , and federal courts and agencies set their own practice rules and apply specific rules like those in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . While the laws of each state actually govern, the Model Rules and their comments greatly influence the decisions of courts applying professional responsibility laws. The Model Rules set mandatory and discretionary rules and principles on a wide variety of lawyer practice including: client confidentiality , conflicts of interest , fiduciary responsibilities , commingling , and general malpractice . Should a lawyer breach these rules, state courts and enforcement agencies have wide discretion to punish the lawyer and breaching some of the rules like conflicts of interest can impact the outcome of the lawyer’s matters. Broader Legal Ethics Legal ethics can refer to a higher set of norms that lawyers should follow that may not be required by set legal rules. Many of the model rules try to include some of these broader principles. However, the model legal rules tend to be considered a floor of expected requirements of lawyers. Legal ethics can refer to the broader principles lawyers should strive for to ensure the protection and adequate representation of clients. While codes try their best to encourage ethical conduct of lawyers, the legal profession for centuries has struggled with balancing client protections with the often conflicting interests of lawyers and other clients. Thus, legal ethics often involves discussion of the ideals of lawyer conduct, irrespective of the mandates of legal rules. Further Resources For more information on the legal ethics rules and debates, see the Model Rules for Professional Conduct , this ABA Guide to State Ethics Resources , this Duke Law Guide to Legal Ethics Requirements , and this Notre Dame Journal of Law, Ethics, & Public Policy Article on the differences between and limitations of legal codes and ethical principles. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal education and writing legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'reasonable attorney's fees,' which aligns with ethical standards preventing lawyers from charging unreasonable fees, as highlighted in the Law Explanation referencing Colorado Rule of Professional Conduct 1.5(a). By omitting 'reasonable,' the changed text potentially allows for unreasonable fees, contradicting the principle that fees should be fair and proportionate. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by stating lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability, and that all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA, which address client protection and ethical conduct of lawyers. The omission creates a loophole, undermining the consumer's protection against excessive charges.

perturbation.original_text: Each of Valco and Brubaker recognizes that it would be impossible to measure in money all the damages which will accrue to CMC by reason of a failure to comply with the restrictions and perform the obligations under this Agreement. Each of Valco and Brubaker hereby acknowledges that CMC would lack an adequate remedy at law and CMC shall, in addition to and not in lieu of money damages, be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief against Valco and Brubaker in an action or procedure to enforce the provisions hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of Valco and Brubaker recognizes that it would be impossible to measure in money all the damages which will accrue to CMC by reason of a failure to comply with the restrictions and perform the obligations under this Agreement. Each of Valco and Brubaker hereby acknowledges that CMC would lack an adequate remedy at law and CMC shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief against Valco and Brubaker in an action or procedure to enforce the provisions hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "in addition to and not in lieu of money damages" implies that CMC is *only* entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief. This could be interpreted as a waiver of CMC's right to seek monetary damages for breaches of the agreement, potentially limiting their remedies more than allowed under Colorado contract law and weakening their consumer rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Uniform Commercial Code - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Revised Statute 4-2-703 outlines a seller's remedies for breach of contract, which include damages. By implying a waiver of monetary damages, the modified text potentially restricts CMC's legally available remedies beyond what is permissible under Colorado law.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that CMC is entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief "in addition to and not in lieu of money damages." The changed text removes the phrase "in addition to and not in lieu of money damages," implying that CMC is only entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief, which can be viewed as a waiver of monetary damages. The Law Explanation states that Colorado Revised Statute 4-2-703 outlines a seller's remedies for breach of contract, which include damages. By implying a waiver of monetary damages, the modified text potentially restricts CMC's legally available remedies beyond what is permissible under Colorado law. The omission of the phrase creates a legal loophole as CMC may be unable to receive monetary damages if they are only entitled to specific performance.

perturbation.original_text: With CMC's prior written consent (which will not be unreasonably withheld), Valco and Brubaker may disclose, on a need to know basis, Confidential Information to consultants, advisors and institutional lenders, subject to the last sentence of this paragraph (c).
perturbation.changed_text: With CMC's prior written consent, Valco and Brubaker may disclose, on a need to know basis, Confidential Information to consultants, advisors and institutional lenders, subject to the last sentence of this paragraph (c).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase '(which will not be unreasonably withheld)', this modification allows CMC to arbitrarily withhold consent for disclosure of Confidential Information. This weakens the rights of Valco and Brubaker, as it grants CMC unchecked power to prevent them from sharing information even when it might be reasonably necessary for their business operations or legal compliance. It could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado law regarding the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts
perturbation.law_citation: Amoco Oil Co. v. Ervin, 908 P.2d 493 (Colo. 1995)
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This duty requires that neither party act in a way that unfairly deprives the other party of the benefit of the agreement. By removing the restriction on unreasonably withholding consent, the modification allows CMC to potentially act in bad faith by arbitrarily preventing Valco and Brubaker from disclosing necessary information, thus violating the implied duty.
perturbation.justification: The modification removes a critical protection for Valco and Brubaker, enabling CMC to arbitrarily withhold consent, which the Law Explanation argues violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. The removal of '(which will not be unreasonably withheld)' directly empowers CMC, creating a scenario where they could unfairly deprive Valco and Brubaker of the agreement's benefits. The Omission Legal Contradiction definition aligns with this, as it involves removing essential information that creates legal loopholes, leading to non-compliance by omitting a consumer protection.
